original_abstract:
This study presents a comprehensive evaluation of the chemical profile, antioxidant capacity, and biological activities of *Prangos ferulacea* aqueous extract. Qualitative analysis via HPLC and FTIR identified $p$-coumaric acid as the predominant phenolic constituent, alongside key flavonoids such as catechin, rutin, myricetin, and kaempferol. Quantitative assessment revealed significant totals for phenolic (202.04 ± 5.46 mg GAE/g) and flavonoid (1,909.46 ± 13 µg QE/g) contents, complemented by notable beta-carotene levels.

The extract exhibited robust antioxidant potential across multiple assays (DPPH, ABTS, and FRAP), indicating its efficacy as a radical scavenger. Significant cytotoxic activity was observed against the HT29 cancer cell line, with cell viability decreasing in a dose-dependent manner ($IC_{50} = 82.15 \pm 0.02 \mu g/mL$). Furthermore, the extract demonstrated broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties against several foodborne pathogens, with *Listeria monocytogenes* identified as the most susceptible strain. Morphological analysis using SEM and CLSM confirmed that the extract induced severe structural damage and cell lysis in *L. monocytogenes*. Molecular investigation revealed that the extract significantly downregulated the expression of several critical virulence and biofilm-related genes, including *sigB*, *prfA*, *hly*, and *plcB*. These findings highlight the potential of *P. ferulacea* as a natural source of bioactive compounds for pharmaceutical and food safety applications, particularly in mitigating *Listeria* infections.
humanized_abstract:
The aim of the current study was to investigate the profile of chemical constituents, antioxidants as well as some biological activities of the aqueous extract of *Prangos ferulacea* obtained from Kermanshah province. The qualitative determination of some chemical constituents of *P* . *f* aqueous extract revealed by HPLC and FTIR that $p$ -coumaric acid was the main identified compounds in the aqueous extract along with some important flavonoids such as catechin, rutin, myricetin and kaempferol. The quantitative analysis revealed that aqueous extract of *P* . *f* exhibited very high amount of phenolic compounds (202.04 ± 5.46 mg GAE/g) and flavonoids (1909.46 ± 13µg QE/g) as well as beta-carotene.

Extract of medicinal plant Piper ferulacea (P. ferulacea) was characterized by its strong antioxidant activities revealed by using DPPH, ABTS, and FRAP assays due to its strong scavenging ability of free radicals. In addition, our results showed strong cytotoxic effects of P. ferulacea extract on human colorectal adenocarcinoma cancer cell lines (HT29) by evaluating the half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) values; $IC_{50} = 82.15 \pm 0.02 \mu g/mL$. Results of antimicrobial studies on some foodborne pathogens clearly indicated that P. ferulacea extract possessed broader spectrum of antibacterial activity where Listeria monocytogenes was shown to be highly susceptible than other tested microorganisms. Structural damages to the surface of Listeria monocytogenes caused by P. ferulacea extract were shown using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). Expression of certain important virulence and biofilm-associated genes such as sigB, prfA, hly, and plcB in Listeria monocytogenes revealed that P. ferulacea extract could potentially be used as an alternative candidate for the upscaling and commercialization of bioactive molecules for medicinal and food industries, particularly for management of Listeria.
